facts.text: The Flash first appeared in Flash Comics #1 in January 1940, created by writer Gardner Fox and artist Harry Lampert, making him one of the earliest DC Comics superheroes.
Jay Garrick was the original Flash of the Golden Age, a college student who gained super speed after inhaling hard water vapors in a laboratory accident.
Jay Garrick's iconic winged helmet was inspired by the Roman god Mercury, the messenger of the gods known for his incredible speed.
Barry Allen debuted in Showcase #4 in October 1956, ushering in the Silver Age of Comics and revitalizing the superhero genre.
Barry Allen was a forensic scientist working for the Central City Police Department who was struck by lightning and doused with chemicals, giving him super speed.
The name 'Barry Allen' was derived from two talk show hosts: Barry Gray and Steve Allen.
Robert Kanigher and Carmine Infantino created the Barry Allen version of The Flash, redesigning the character with a sleek red costume and lightning bolt emblem.
Wally West first appeared in The Flash #110 in 1959 as Kid Flash, Barry Allen's nephew who gained powers in a nearly identical accident.
Wally West became the third Flash after Barry Allen's death in Crisis on Infinite Earths #8 in 1985, carrying the mantle for over 20 years in comics.
Bart Allen, the grandson of Barry Allen from the 30th century, first appeared in Flash #92 in 1994 under the name Impulse before later becoming Kid Flash and then The Flash.
The Speed Force is an extra-dimensional energy source that grants all speedsters their power, first introduced conceptually in the comics by Mark Waid in the 1990s.
Mark Waid's run on The Flash from 1992 to 2000 is considered one of the definitive takes on the character, establishing much of the Speed Force mythology.
Geoff Johns' run on The Flash from 2000 to 2005 revitalized the Rogues Gallery and deepened the lore of the Flash legacy.
The Flash's top speed in comics has varied wildly, but he has been shown to exceed the speed of light and even outrun death itself.
In The Flash #138, Wally West ran so fast he entered the Speed Force dimension, establishing that speedsters could be absorbed into this energy field.
The Flash can vibrate his molecules at such high frequencies that he can phase through solid matter, a technique he uses frequently in both comics and television.
"My name is Barry Allen and I am the fastest man alive." — The opening narration of The Flash TV series became one of the most recognizable intros in superhero television.
The Flash TV series premiered on The CW on October 7, 2014, with Grant Gustin starring as Barry Allen.
Grant Gustin first appeared as Barry Allen in Season 2 of Arrow before getting his own spinoff series.
The Flash TV series ran for nine seasons from 2014 to 2023, making it one of the longest-running Arrowverse shows.
The Flash TV pilot was watched by 4.8 million viewers, making it The CW's most-watched premiere in five years at the time.
"Run, Barry, run!" — This iconic phrase, spoken by multiple characters throughout The Flash TV series, became the show's unofficial catchphrase.
Tom Cavanagh played multiple versions of Harrison Wells across The Flash TV series, showcasing a different Wells from a different Earth nearly every season.
"It is I who is the Reverse." — Eobard Thawne's reveal as the Reverse-Flash in Season 1 of The Flash TV series was one of the show's most shocking moments.
The Reverse-Flash, also known as Professor Zoom, is Eobard Thawne from the 25th century who became obsessed with The Flash and eventually became his greatest enemy.
Eobard Thawne first appeared in The Flash #139 in September 1963, created by John Broome and Carmine Infantino.
"You can't lock up the darkness." — Zoom's chilling declaration in Season 2 of The Flash became one of the most quoted villain lines from the series.
Hunter Zolomon, also known as Zoom, is a different character from Eobard Thawne despite both being called Reverse-Flash at various points in comics and TV.
Captain Cold, real name Leonard Snart, is one of Flash's most iconic rogues. He wields a cold gun that can freeze objects to absolute zero.
Wentworth Miller portrayed Captain Cold in The Flash TV series and later became a series regular on DC's Legends of Tomorrow.
"There are no strings on me." — Leonard Snart's declaration of independence after betraying his father echoed his complex antihero arc across the Arrowverse.
The Rogues Gallery is the collective name for Flash's recurring villains, known for having a strict code of honor among thieves.
Captain Cold established the Rogues' rules: no killing unless absolutely necessary, no drugs, and no fighting other Rogues' battles.
Gorilla Grodd is a hyper-intelligent telepathic gorilla and one of Flash's most powerful enemies, first appearing in The Flash #106 in 1959.
Gorilla City is a hidden civilization of super-intelligent gorillas in Africa, home to Gorilla Grodd in DC Comics lore.
Mirror Master can travel through and manipulate reflective surfaces, making him one of the most creative and dangerous of the Flash's rogues.
Sam Scudder was the original Mirror Master, while Evan McCulloch became the second, with the latter actually able to enter the Mirror Dimension.
Weather Wizard, real name Mark Mardon, uses a wand that controls weather patterns, allowing him to create storms, tornadoes, and lightning.
The Trickster was originally James Jesse, a circus acrobat turned criminal who used joke-themed gadgets, predating The Joker in concept by using similar gimmicks.
Mark Hamill played the Trickster in the 1990 Flash TV series and reprised the role in The CW's Flash series, making him one of few actors to play the same DC villain across different shows.
Mark Hamill's portrayal of the Trickster directly influenced his casting as the voice of The Joker in Batman: The Animated Series.
Heat Wave, real name Mick Rory, is Captain Cold's partner and wields a heat gun that serves as the perfect counterpart to Cold's freeze gun.
Dominic Purcell played Heat Wave in the Arrowverse, reuniting with his Prison Break co-star Wentworth Miller.
The Pied Piper, Hartley Rathaway, was one of the first openly gay supervillains in DC Comics and later reformed to become a hero.
Golden Glider, Lisa Snart, is Captain Cold's sister who originally used ice skates that could create their own ice and a jewel that could project illusions.
Crisis on Infinite Earths (1985-1986) featured the death of Barry Allen, who sacrificed himself to destroy the Anti-Monitor's antimatter cannon by running faster than he ever had before.
Barry Allen's death in Crisis on Infinite Earths was one of the most significant superhero deaths in comics history, and he remained dead for 23 years before returning in 2008.
"The Flash died as he lived — running." — This narration from Crisis on Infinite Earths #8 became one of the most quoted lines in DC Comics history.
Flashpoint is a 2011 DC Comics crossover event written by Geoff Johns where Barry Allen travels back in time to save his mother, creating an alternate dystopian timeline.
In Flashpoint, Thomas Wayne became Batman instead of Bruce, Martha Wayne became The Joker, and Aquaman and Wonder Woman were at war with each other.
The Flashpoint storyline led directly to the New 52 reboot of the entire DC Universe in September 2011.
The Flash TV series adapted the Flashpoint storyline in the Season 3 premiere, showing Barry living in an alternate timeline after saving his mother.
"Every time I've messed with the timeline, it's only gotten worse." — Barry Allen's realization about the consequences of time travel became a recurring theme in The Flash TV series.
The Flash can create speed mirages by moving so fast that he appears to be in multiple places at once, essentially creating afterimages of himself.
Infinite Mass Punch is a technique where The Flash accelerates to near-light speed, increasing his relativistic mass to deliver a punch with the force of a dwarf star.
The Flash can throw lightning bolts by generating static electricity through friction as he runs, a power frequently depicted in both comics and the TV series.
Speed thinking allows The Flash to process information and think at superhuman speeds, essentially making seconds feel like hours from his perspective.
The Flash 2023 movie starred Ezra Miller as Barry Allen and featured both Ben Affleck and Michael Keaton as different versions of Batman.
The Flash (2023) movie used the Flashpoint storyline as its basis, with Barry traveling back in time to prevent his mother's murder.
Michael Keaton's return as Batman in The Flash (2023) marked his first appearance in the role since Batman Returns in 1992, a 31-year gap.
The Flash (2023) movie featured a cameo-filled sequence showing various DC characters across the multiverse, including Nicolas Cage as Superman.
The Flash (2023) film was directed by Andy Muschietti, known for directing the It horror films.
The Flash (2023) had a reported budget of approximately $200-220 million, making it one of the most expensive DC films produced.
Sasha Calle made her DC debut as Supergirl in The Flash (2023), portraying a Kryptonian who had been imprisoned by the Russian government.
The 1990 Flash TV series starred John Wesley Shipp as Barry Allen and ran for one season on CBS, earning a cult following despite its cancellation.
John Wesley Shipp returned to play Barry Allen's father Henry Allen in The CW's Flash series, and later played Jay Garrick, the Earth-3 Flash.
"I'm not a hero. I'm just a guy who got struck by lightning." — Barry Allen's humble self-assessment in the early episodes of The Flash TV series.
The Speed Force was depicted as a literal dimension in The Flash TV series, often visualized as a stormy, lightning-filled void.
In Season 3 of The Flash TV show, Savitar was revealed to be a time remnant of Barry Allen from the future, making Flash his own worst enemy.
"I am the future Flash." — Savitar's declaration carried a deliberate double meaning, as the sentence could be read as 'I am the Future Flash' or 'I am the future, Flash.'
Cisco Ramon, played by Carlos Valdes, was Barry's best friend and tech support on The Flash TV series, later developing metahuman powers as Vibe.
Caitlin Snow, played by Danielle Panabaker, served as S.T.A.R. Labs' resident bioengineer and eventually developed a Killer Frost alter ego on The Flash.
"To me, you've been dead for centuries." — Eobard Thawne's cold dismissal of Cisco Ramon in Season 1 remains one of the most chilling villain moments in the Arrowverse.
Harrison Wells was originally a scientist murdered by Eobard Thawne, who then assumed Wells' identity to build the particle accelerator and create The Flash.
The S.T.A.R. Labs particle accelerator explosion in The Flash TV series was the event that created most of Central City's metahumans, including Barry Allen.
Joe West, played by Jesse L. Martin, served as Barry Allen's adoptive father and a detective with the Central City Police on The Flash TV series.
Iris West, played by Candice Patton, was Barry Allen's love interest and eventually his wife on The Flash TV series, also becoming the leader of Team Flash.
"We are The Flash." — Iris West-Allen's controversial declaration in Season 4 sparked extensive debate among fans about shared superhero identity.
In DC Comics, Iris West-Allen is a reporter who originally worked for Picture News in Central City before learning Barry's secret identity.
The Flash's costume ring is a special device that can compress and store his entire costume, allowing him to change into The Flash at a moment's notice.
In the comics, Barry Allen designed his costume to be compressed into a ring using a special chemical that would cause the suit to expand when exposed to air.
The Flash's iconic red costume with the lightning bolt chest emblem was designed by Carmine Infantino and has remained largely consistent since 1956.
Jay Garrick's original Flash costume had no mask, just a winged helmet, making him one of the few major superheroes whose identity was publicly known.
"Flash of Two Worlds" in The Flash #123 (1961) introduced the concept of the DC Multiverse by having Barry Allen vibrate to a parallel Earth and meet Jay Garrick.
The Flash of Two Worlds story established that the Golden Age DC characters existed on Earth-Two while the Silver Age characters lived on Earth-One.
The concept of the DC Multiverse, one of comics' most important storytelling devices, was essentially invented in a Flash comic.
The Cosmic Treadmill is a device invented by Barry Allen that allows speedsters to travel through time with precision by running on it at specific speeds.
Without the Cosmic Treadmill, speedsters risk becoming lost in the timestream when attempting to time travel, as demonstrated multiple times in the comics.
Wally West was initially slower than Barry Allen and could not vibrate through solid objects without causing them to explode, limitations he eventually overcame.
Wally West eventually surpassed Barry Allen in speed, becoming the fastest Flash and one of the fastest beings in the DC Universe.
"I'm Wally West. I'm the fastest man alive." — Wally West's assertion of his identity as The Flash during his tenure carrying the mantle.
Bart Allen aged rapidly due to his hypermetabolism and was raised in a virtual reality simulation in the 30th century before traveling back to the present.
Bart Allen briefly served as The Flash in 2006-2007 before being killed by the Rogues, though he was later resurrected.
Max Mercury, originally called Quicksilver in the 1940s, is known as the Zen Master of Speed and served as a mentor to several speedsters.
Jesse Quick, daughter of Golden Age heroes Johnny Quick and Liberty Belle, can access the Speed Force by reciting her father's mathematical formula: 3X2(9YZ)4A.
The Black Flash is the Speed Force's equivalent of Death, appearing to speedsters when their time has come, depicted as a decayed zombie-like version of The Flash.
In The Flash TV series, the Black Flash appeared as a time wraith-like enforcer of the Speed Force, hunting down aberrations in the timeline.
"Now who's the villain, Flash? Now who's the villain?" — Eobard Thawne's taunting question to Barry Allen exemplified the complex moral dynamics between hero and villain.
The Negative Speed Force was created by Eobard Thawne as a corrupted version of the Speed Force, giving him powers that directly counter those of The Flash.
Professor Zoom can alter his own personal timeline, essentially making changes to history that only affect him, a power unique among speedsters.
Eobard Thawne was originally Barry Allen's biggest fan from the future who went insane after discovering he was destined to become Flash's greatest enemy.
The Reverse-Flash's costume is the inverse of Barry Allen's — yellow where Flash is red, and red where Flash is yellow, symbolizing their opposing natures.
In the comics, Eobard Thawne murdered Barry Allen's mother Nora Allen and framed Barry's father Henry for the crime, setting up a decades-long tragedy.
The Flash can speed-read, absorbing entire books in seconds, though in some versions the knowledge fades quickly after he slows down.
Speed healing is one of Flash's abilities — he can heal from injuries much faster than normal humans, though the rate varies depending on the severity.
The Flash's metabolism is so fast that he needs to consume enormous amounts of calories to sustain his speed, often depicted eating massive quantities of food.
In The Flash TV series, Cisco named many of the villains, creating the tradition of metahuman codenames that became a running gag throughout the show.
The Arrowverse crossover event "Crisis on Infinite Earths" (2019-2020) featured Grant Gustin's Flash meeting Ezra Miller's movie Flash in a brief but memorable scene.
The CW's Crisis on Infinite Earths crossover adapted the classic comic storyline across five episodes spanning Arrow, The Flash, Supergirl, Batwoman, and Legends of Tomorrow.
In the CW's Crisis on Infinite Earths, the Flash of Earth-90 (John Wesley Shipp) sacrificed himself on the antimatter cannon, mirroring Barry Allen's death in the original comics.
"The real Flash has a chin strap." — A humorous meta-reference made during the brief Ezra Miller and Grant Gustin crossover in Crisis on Infinite Earths.
The Flash can create time remnants — copies of himself pulled from slightly in the future — but this creates paradoxes that attract time wraiths.
Time wraiths are Speed Force enforcers that hunt speedsters who abuse time travel, introduced in The Flash TV series as decayed, wraith-like creatures.
The Flash has outrun the Black Racer, Death itself, in the comics, demonstrating that his speed has virtually no upper limit.
In Final Crisis, Barry Allen outran the Omega Sanction and the Black Racer simultaneously, leading Death to strike Darkseid instead.
The Flash can lend and borrow speed from other objects and people, slowing them down or speeding them up through his connection to the Speed Force.
Keystone City is Jay Garrick's home city, while Central City is Barry Allen's, and in the comics, they are twin cities connected by a bridge.
Central City is often depicted as being in Missouri, while Keystone City is in Kansas, separated by the Missouri River.
The Flash Museum in Central City is a museum dedicated to The Flash in the comics, containing memorabilia, villain trophies, and a Hall of Villains.
"Every kid dreams of being The Flash. Every kid dreams of being the fastest person alive." — Grant Gustin reflected on the universal appeal of the character during interviews.
The Flash's ability to vibrate through walls was first depicted in the Silver Age comics and has become one of the character's most signature abilities.
When The Flash vibrates through electronic devices, he can cause them to explode, a side effect he has learned to control over time.
The Flash can create vortexes by spinning his arms at super speed, generating powerful wind tunnels that can extinguish fires or redirect projectiles.
In Justice League: The Flashpoint Paradox (2013), the animated movie adapted the Flashpoint storyline with voice actor Justin Chambers as Barry Allen.
The Flash appeared in the Justice League animated series (2001-2004) voiced by Michael Rosenbaum, who portrayed a more comedic, Wally West version of the character.
"I'm the fastest man alive. I'm The Flash." — Wally West's declaration in the Justice League animated series, delivered with characteristic charm by Michael Rosenbaum.
In the Justice League Unlimited episode "Divided We Fall," Wally West tapped into the full Speed Force for the first time, nearly being absorbed into it.
The Justice League animated series' Flash was one of the most beloved versions of the character, balancing humor with genuine heroism.
In Young Justice, Wally West served as Kid Flash and was a founding member of the team alongside Robin, Aqualad, Superboy, and Miss Martian.
Wally West's death in the Young Justice Season 2 finale, where he was consumed by the Speed Force while helping to stop the Reach's magnetic field disruptor, was a devastating moment for fans.
Young Justice brought Bart Allen into the show as Impulse, a time traveler from a dystopian future who came back to prevent a catastrophe.
The Flash has appeared in nearly every DC animated movie and series, making him one of the most adapted DC characters outside of Batman and Superman.
In the animated film Justice League: War (2014), the Flash was voiced by Christopher Gorham and was depicted as a founding member of the Justice League.
The Flash appeared in the Batman: The Brave and the Bold animated series, with various versions of the character making appearances.
In Injustice: Gods Among Us, The Flash initially sided with Superman's authoritarian regime before eventually defecting to Batman's insurgency.
The Injustice comics explored a version of The Flash tormented by guilt over supporting Superman's tyrannical rule, adding moral complexity to the character.
The Flash is a playable character in every Injustice fighting game, with move sets that emphasize his speed through rapid combo attacks and time-slow mechanics.
In the LEGO DC Super Heroes animated films, The Flash is often portrayed as the comic relief character, leaning into his fast-talking personality.
DC Super Hero Girls featured a teenage version of Barry Allen as The Flash, adapting the character for a younger audience.
"Life doesn't give us purpose. We give life purpose." — Barry Allen's philosophical outlook in The Flash TV series reflected the character's optimistic worldview.
The Flash has been a member of the Justice League in nearly every incarnation of the team, making him one of the most consistent members alongside Batman, Superman, and Wonder Woman.
The Flash's friendship with Green Lantern (Hal Jordan) is one of the most iconic bromances in DC Comics, with the two frequently teaming up.
Barry Allen and Hal Jordan's friendship has been depicted across comics, animation, and in the Justice League movie, where they share a mutual respect.
The Flash and Superman have raced each other multiple times in comics, with the results varying — though The Flash has won the majority of their races.
In The Flash #49 (2018), Barry Allen definitively beat Superman in a race, with the comic establishing that The Flash is faster because speed is his entire purpose.
"Those races were for charity, Clark." — The Flash's iconic response to Superman, implying he had been holding back during their previous races.
The Flash's lightning bolt emblem has been colored white, yellow, and red at various points across different versions of the character.
Wally West's Flash costume was originally identical to Barry's but later evolved to have a darker red color and different eye design to distinguish the two.
Kid Flash's original costume was a reverse color scheme of The Flash's — yellow where Flash was red and red where Flash was yellow.
Impulse's original costume was white and red, reflecting Bart Allen's futuristic origins and distinguishing him from previous speedsters.
The Flash family includes Jay Garrick, Barry Allen, Wally West, Bart Allen, Jesse Quick, Max Mercury, XS, and several other speedsters connected through the Speed Force.
XS, real name Jenni Ognats, is Barry Allen's granddaughter and a member of the Legion of Super-Heroes in the 30th century.
The Tornado Twins, Don and Dawn Allen, are Barry Allen's children from the future who also possessed super speed.
Nora West-Allen, introduced in Season 5 of The Flash TV series, was Barry and Iris's daughter from the future who traveled back in time, going by the name XS.
Jessica Parker Kennedy portrayed Nora West-Allen/XS on The Flash TV series, adding a new generational layer to the Flash family.
"What did future Barry Allen do that was so terrible that it inspired Thawne's hatred?" This question drove much of the Reverse-Flash mythology for decades.
In the comics, Eobard Thawne's origin reveals that the future Barry Allen repeatedly and ruthlessly stopped Thawne's crimes, eventually driving him to obsession and madness.
The Speed Force has been depicted as sentient in both comics and the TV series, sometimes taking human form to communicate with speedsters.
In The Flash TV series Season 7, the Speed Force took the form of Barry's mother Nora Allen, played by Michelle Harrison, adding an emotional dimension to the concept.
The Still Force, Sage Force, and Strength Force were introduced as additional cosmic forces in the comics, each representing different fundamental aspects of reality.
Godspeed, real name August Heart, was Barry Allen's friend at the CCPD who became a speedster villain obsessed with gaining more speed in the comics.
Godspeed appeared in The Flash TV series Season 7, where multiple Godspeed clones terrorized Central City.
The Thinker, Clifford DeVoe, served as the main villain of The Flash Season 4, using his superintelligence rather than speed to challenge Barry.
Season 4 of The Flash introduced the concept of dark matter metahumans created on a bus during Barry's exit from the Speed Force.
Cicada was the main villain of The Flash Season 5, a grief-stricken man who used a lightning bolt-shaped dagger that dampened metahuman powers.
"You and I have been doing this dance for a long time, Flash." — Eobard Thawne acknowledging the eternal rivalry between himself and Barry Allen.
Mirror Monarch, Eva McCulloch, served as the main antagonist of The Flash Season 6, trapped in and eventually controlling the Mirror Dimension.
The Flash Season 1 finale featured Barry running fast enough to create a wormhole and travel back in time to the night his mother was murdered.
Eddie Thawne, Eobard's ancestor played by Rick Cosnett, sacrificed himself in the Season 1 finale to erase Eobard from existence by killing himself.
"The man in the yellow suit. I've been searching for him all my life." — Barry Allen's obsession with finding his mother's killer drove the entire first season of The Flash.
The Flash TV series Season 2 introduced the multiverse to the Arrowverse, with breaches opening to Earth-2 and Jay Garrick's world.
Zoom (Hunter Zolomon) in The Flash Season 2 was revealed to have been posing as Jay Garrick, in a twist that shocked viewers.
Tony Todd provided the menacing voice for Zoom in Season 2 of The Flash, while Teddy Sears played the character in human form.
The Flash Season 2 introduced the concept of Earth-2 doppelgangers, showing alternate versions of familiar characters with different personalities and roles.
Killer Frost became a fan-favorite character on The Flash, evolving from villain to antihero to full hero over the course of the series.
"You can't save everyone, Barry." — A recurring lesson Barry Allen had to learn throughout The Flash TV series as he grappled with the limits of his powers.
The Flash has been featured in numerous DC video games, including Injustice, DC Universe Online, LEGO Batman, and Mortal Kombat vs. DC Universe.
In DC Universe Online, players can choose super-speed as their movement type, essentially becoming their own version of The Flash.
The Flash has his own dedicated video game, The Flash (1991) for the Sega Master System, which was one of the earliest superhero platformers.
In Mortal Kombat vs. DC Universe (2008), The Flash was a playable character who used super-speed combos and phasing abilities.
The Flash appeared as a playable character in Multiversus, Warner Bros.' platform fighting game, using speed-based attacks and combos.
Speed Force lightning changes color based on the speedster's emotional state and connection — Barry's is yellow, Wally's is often white or yellow, and Thawne's is red.
In the New 52, Barry Allen's origin was updated so that the Speed Force was actually created by Barry himself when he was struck by lightning, making him its source.
The Flash: Rebirth (2009) by Geoff Johns and Ethan Van Sciver brought Barry Allen back from the dead after 23 years, reestablishing him as the primary Flash.
"Every second is a gift." — Barry Allen's perspective after returning from the Speed Force in Flash: Rebirth, reflecting on his appreciation for the moments between the running.
The Flash War storyline (2018) pitted Barry Allen against Wally West as they were manipulated by Hunter Zolomon into breaking the Force Barrier.
Heroes in Crisis (2018-2019) controversially depicted Wally West having a mental breakdown at Sanctuary, a rehabilitation facility for traumatized heroes.
Wally West's redemption arc after Heroes in Crisis was completed in Flash Forward and Infinite Frontier, eventually making him the primary Flash again.
The Flash has operated as both a solo hero and a team player, reflecting the character's versatility and importance in the broader DC Universe.
The Flash's rogues are unique among superhero villain galleries because they often work together as a team and follow a code of conduct.
Captain Boomerang, a Flash villain, became more widely known through his appearances in the Suicide Squad comics and films.
The Top, real name Roscoe Dillon, could spin at incredible speeds and later developed telekinetic powers, making him one of Flash's more unusual rogues.
Abra Kadabra is a villain from the 64th century who uses advanced technology that appears as magic to people of the present day.
Doctor Alchemy uses the Philosopher's Stone to transmute elements, serving as one of the more science-themed villains in Flash's rogues gallery.
The Turtle, also known as the Slowest Man Alive, can steal kinetic energy from objects and people, making him the conceptual opposite of The Flash.
Murmur is a serial killer Flash villain who cut out his own tongue and sewed his mouth shut to prevent himself from confessing to his crimes.
Girder, real name Tony Woodward, is a metahuman whose body is made of living steel, making him one of Flash's more physically imposing adversaries.
Tar Pit is a Flash villain who can project his consciousness into molten tar, creating a body of living asphalt that is nearly indestructible.
The Rainbow Raider could create solid light constructs and manipulate emotions through color, making him a visually distinctive but underrated Flash villain.
"Sometimes the only way to move forward is to revisit the things in your past that were holding you back." — Barry Allen's reflection on confronting his trauma in The Flash TV series.
The Flash has been adapted into a stage show at Six Flags theme parks, where he races through the park meeting visitors.
Central City in The Flash TV series was primarily filmed in Vancouver, British Columbia, with the production using various locations around the city.
The Flash TV series' special effects team won multiple awards for their work on the show's super-speed sequences and lightning effects.
The iconic Flash ring from the comics was eventually incorporated into The Flash TV series, with Barry receiving a suit-compressing ring in later seasons.
The Elongated Man, Ralph Dibny, was introduced in The Flash Season 4, played by Hartley Sawyer, adding another hero to Team Flash.
Chester P. Runk, played by Brandon McKnight, joined Team Flash in Season 6 as the new tech genius after Cisco's departure.
Allegra Garcia, played by Kayla Compton, was a metahuman with ultraviolet light powers who became a key member of Team Flash in later seasons.
"Stars. Every one of them. Every single one. The beginning of a new universe." — Barry Allen witnessing the Big Bang while running through the Speed Force.
The Flash can generate enough friction while running to melt steel and has used this ability tactically in numerous comic book battles.
Speed Force aura protects The Flash and anything he carries from the effects of friction, air resistance, and kinetic impacts while running at high speeds.
The Flash can perceive events that occur in attoseconds — quintillionths of a second — making his perception speed nearly incomprehensible.
In the comics, Barry Allen once evacuated an entire city of 532,000 people in 0.00001 microseconds, carrying each person individually to safety.
The Flash can run on water by moving fast enough that his feet don't have time to break the surface tension, a feat depicted in nearly every Flash adaptation.
The Flash can run up vertical surfaces and even upside down by generating enough speed to counteract gravity through momentum.
The Flash created a tornado powerful enough to counter one created by Weather Wizard by running in the opposite direction at super speed.
"Speed is the one thing that I know how to do, and if I can't do that, who am I?" — Barry Allen grappling with his identity when he temporarily lost his powers.
The Flash's connection to the Speed Force means he ages more slowly than normal humans, potentially extending his lifespan significantly.
In some comic storylines, the Speed Force exists outside of normal time and space, making it accessible to speedsters across all eras and dimensions.
Daniel West, Iris's brother in the New 52, became a Reverse-Flash who could travel backward through time by absorbing the Speed Force from a monorail engine.
Meena Dhawan, also known as Fast Track, was a S.T.A.R. Labs scientist who gained super speed and briefly served as Barry Allen's love interest and partner.
Avery Ho is a Chinese-American speedster who became The Flash of China as part of the Justice League of China.
Wallace West, Wally's younger cousin introduced in the New 52, became the new Kid Flash, adding another generation to the Flash family.
The Flash has traveled to the beginning and end of time, witnessed the Big Bang, and run to the edge of the universe in various comic storylines.
"Not God. Grodd." — Gorilla Grodd's iconic self-correction became a memorable catchphrase for the telepathic gorilla villain.
Gorilla Grodd has been depicted in The Flash TV series as a recurring threat, with the show using impressive CGI to bring the telepathic gorilla to life.
King Shark, a humanoid shark metahuman, became a recurring character on The Flash TV series, eventually being reformed and becoming an ally.
The Flash TV series featured 184 episodes across its nine-season run, making it one of the most prolific superhero television shows ever produced.
Grant Gustin performed many of his own stunts on The Flash TV series, working extensively with the show's stunt team to create authentic running sequences.
The Flash suit evolved significantly throughout the TV series, starting as a leather-based design and eventually becoming a more comics-accurate bright red suit.
"I've been running my whole life. But I never really felt like I was going anywhere until I was running with you." — Barry Allen's romantic declaration to Iris West.
Barry and Iris's wedding on The Flash TV series was interrupted by a crossover villain attack during the "Crisis on Earth-X" event.
The Flash TV series featured numerous musical references, including a full musical crossover episode with Supergirl called "Duet" in Season 3.
John Wesley Shipp wore the original 1990 Flash suit, which was created by legendary costume designer Robert Short and cost approximately $100,000 to build.
The 1990 Flash TV series featured Danny Elfman's iconic theme music, which is still considered one of the best superhero TV themes ever composed.
Amanda Pays appeared in both the 1990 Flash series and the CW Flash series, playing different characters named Tina McGee in both shows.
"Be the Flash." — Oliver Queen's encouragement to Barry Allen before his sacrifice, acknowledging the importance of the Flash legacy.
The Flash Season 6 was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, forcing the season to end three episodes early with an abrupt conclusion.
The Flash Season 9, the final season, was a shortened 13-episode run that wrapped up Barry Allen's story on The CW.
The final episode of The Flash TV series aired on May 24, 2023, concluding nine years of storytelling for Grant Gustin's Barry Allen.
Eric Wallace served as showrunner for The Flash from Season 6 through Season 9, guiding the show through its final years.
Andrew Kreisberg and Greg Berlanti co-created The Flash TV series, with Berlanti overseeing the entire Arrowverse franchise.
Geoff Johns, the legendary DC Comics writer, served as an executive producer on The Flash TV series and helped shape its mythology.
The Flash TV series' pilot was directed by David Nutter, known for directing pilots for shows like Game of Thrones and Smallville.
"There's only one thing I know about life. I know some things happen by chance, and some things happen because we make them happen." — Barry Allen on The Flash TV series.
The Flash's Speed Force connection has allowed him to travel between dimensions, accessing parallel Earths and alternate timelines.
In the Arrowverse crossover "Elseworlds," Barry Allen and Oliver Queen swapped identities, with Grant Gustin playing a version of the Green Arrow.
The Monitor, an all-powerful cosmic being, appeared across Arrowverse crossovers as a test and harbinger of the coming Crisis.
The Flash TV series popularized the concept of "metahumans" in mainstream pop culture, a term used throughout the Arrowverse.
Particle accelerator dark matter was established as the primary source of metahuman creation in The Flash TV series' Central City.
The Thinker's chair in The Flash Season 4 was inspired by MODOK's floating chair from Marvel Comics, though adapted for the DC villain.
DeVoe's ability to transfer his consciousness between metahuman bodies made him one of the most unique villains in The Flash TV series.
"Luck is an illusion created by those who can't see the design." — Clifford DeVoe dismissing the role of chance in his carefully orchestrated plans.
The Flash Season 6 introduced the concept of the Mirrorverse, an alternate dimension accessible through reflective surfaces.
Bloodwork, played by Sendhil Ramamurthy, served as the villain for the first half of The Flash Season 6, using dark matter-infected blood.
The Flash #750 (2020) was a landmark anniversary issue featuring contributions from numerous writers and artists spanning the character's 80-year history.
In the DC Rebirth era, Barry Allen and Wally West's relationship was central to the larger mystery of who stole ten years from the DC Universe's timeline.
The Button crossover between Batman and The Flash (2017) connected the DC Rebirth mystery to the Watchmen, revealing Dr. Manhattan's interference.
Doomsday Clock (2017-2019) resolved the Rebirth mystery, with the Speed Force playing a pivotal role in restoring the DCU's lost history.
The Flash has been a core member of the Justice League since the team's first appearance in The Brave and the Bold #28 in 1960.
In the Zack Snyder's Justice League (2021), The Flash's time-reversal sequence during the finale was one of the film's most visually stunning moments.
Ezra Miller's portrayal of Barry Allen in Justice League (2017) depicted a younger, more socially awkward version of the character.
"I just push people and run away." — Ezra Miller's Barry Allen humorously describing his fighting technique in Justice League.
In Zack Snyder's Justice League, Barry Allen used his speed to reverse time and save the entire team, entering the Speed Force in the process.
The Flash's time-travel abilities make him one of the most powerful characters in the DC Universe, capable of altering entire timelines.
In the comics, The Flash has traveled to the 30th century, the 64th century, and even to the end of time itself.
The Flash can steal speed from other objects and people, causing them to slow down or freeze in place while he absorbs their kinetic energy.
Speed lending allows The Flash to give speed to other people or objects, temporarily granting them super speed.
The Flash can create speed duplicates by running so fast he pulls versions of himself from different points in his timeline.
"There is no future without The Flash." — A prophecy about the importance of The Flash in maintaining the timeline and the multiverse.
The Speed Force Storm in the comics created new speedsters around the world, leading to conflicts over who deserved the power.
Eobard Thawne has been killed and resurrected numerous times due to his manipulation of the timeline and the paradoxes surrounding his existence.
The Reverse-Flash is a living paradox — he was erased from existence but preserved by the Speed Force because his death would create a temporal catastrophe.
In The Flash: Rebirth, it was revealed that Eobard Thawne generated the lightning bolt that struck Barry Allen, meaning the villain created his own enemy.
"You want to see something cool?" — Barry Allen's playful attitude about showing off his powers reflects the character's youthful enthusiasm.
The Flash Annual #1 (2018) featured Barry and Iris's vow renewal, one of the most emotionally impactful Flash stories in the Rebirth era.
In the DC Animated Movie Universe, The Flash was voiced by Christopher Gorham across multiple films including Justice League: War and Justice League: Throne of Atlantis.
The Flashpoint Paradox animated film is often cited as one of the best DC animated movies ever made, praised for its mature storytelling and emotional weight.
"Accept the things you cannot change. Have the courage to change the things you can." — Barry Allen quoting the Serenity Prayer in context of his time-travel dilemmas.
The Flash can think fast enough to analyze an entire crime scene in microseconds, making him an incredibly effective forensic scientist.
Barry Allen's day job as a forensic scientist has always been integral to his character, combining detective work with superhero action.
In the comics, Barry Allen was known for being perpetually late despite being the fastest man alive, a charming irony that defined his character.
The CW Flash series addressed Barry's chronic lateness as a character trait in early seasons, playing it for humor before his secret was known.
"Lightning gave me abs?" — Barry Allen's reaction to discovering his transformed physique after waking from the coma caused by the particle accelerator explosion.
The Flash's first live-action appearance was in the 1979 TV special "Legends of the Superheroes," where Rod Haase played Barry Allen.
The Flash appeared in Smallville Season 4, played by Kyle Gallner as Bart Allen/Impulse, who later returned as part of the Justice League.
The Flash has been adapted for stage productions, including a segment in the Justice League Live show that toured various venues.
Carmine Infantino's art style defined the Silver Age Flash, with his dynamic, elongated figures and innovative panel layouts creating the visual language of super-speed in comics.
The Flash #123's cover, depicting Barry Allen and Jay Garrick running side by side, is one of the most iconic and frequently homaged covers in comic book history.
"The Flash: The Fastest Man Alive" comic series (2006-2007) focused on Bart Allen's brief tenure as The Flash.
The Flash has crossed over with nearly every major DC character but has a particularly strong bond with the Green Arrow, dubbed the "hard-traveling heroes" in some runs.
In the Arrowverse, Barry Allen considered Oliver Queen his best friend, and their relationship was central to multiple crossover events.
"Barry, what did you do?" — The shocked reaction from multiple characters whenever Barry altered the timeline, becoming a running joke among fans.
The Elongated Man was historically a Flash supporting character before becoming a Justice League member and starring in his own backup stories.
Ralph Dibny's detective skills rivaled Batman's in some storylines, making him one of the most underrated detectives in the DC Universe.
The Flash's ability to vibrate at different frequencies allows him to become invisible, as he can vibrate his molecular structure to a frequency beyond visible light.
Phasing through objects while carrying another person is extremely difficult for speedsters and requires precise molecular control.
"I wasn't fast enough." — Barry Allen's recurring guilt about not being able to save everyone has been a central theme in both comics and the TV series.
The Flash Vol. 5 by Joshua Williamson ran from 2016 to 2020 and was one of the longest consecutive runs on the title by a single writer.
Joshua Williamson introduced Godspeed, the Speed Force Storm, and the Forces concept during his run on The Flash.
The Flash: Year One storyline in the comics retold Barry Allen's origin, showing his early struggles to understand and control his new abilities.
In Justice League: Doom, the Flash's weakness was exploited by Mirror Master, who attached a bomb to his wrist that would detonate if he slowed below a certain speed.
The Speed Force Sage, an ancient entity within the Speed Force, has appeared in various forms to guide and test speedsters throughout history.
The Flash can perceive and interact with molecules individually, giving him a form of super-perception at the atomic level.
In the comics, The Flash has outrun teleportation, outrun the Speed Force itself, and even outrun the concept of instant transmission.
The Flash's boots contain specialized treads in some comic versions that help him maintain traction at super speed.
"It's not about how fast you go. It's about knowing when to slow down." — A lesson Barry Allen learned about using his powers wisely.
The Flash was one of the founding members of the Justice Society of America, with Jay Garrick serving alongside other Golden Age heroes.
The Justice Society of America, featuring Jay Garrick as The Flash, was the first superhero team in comics, debuting in All Star Comics #3 (1940).
Jay Garrick remained active in DC Comics continuity for over 80 years, making him one of the longest-running characters in comic book history.
In the TV series, Jay Garrick was revealed to be the Earth-3 doppelganger of Barry Allen's father Henry Allen, both played by John Wesley Shipp.
The Speed Force was originally conceived as just a vague energy source but was expanded by Mark Waid into a fully realized cosmic concept.
Mark Waid's "The Return of Barry Allen" storyline is considered one of the greatest Flash stories ever told, redefining Wally West's character.
In "The Return of Barry Allen," the apparent return of Barry Allen was revealed to be Eobard Thawne, who had initially believed himself to be Barry Allen.
"My name is Wally West. I'm the Flash. The fastest man alive. No one will ever forget that." — Wally West's defiant declaration after defeating Thawne in "The Return of Barry Allen."
The Flash: Born to Run by Mark Waid told Wally West's origin story in detail, establishing the character's journey from Kid Flash to The Flash.
Terminal Velocity, another Mark Waid arc, saw Wally West learn to fully tap into the Speed Force, transforming him from a second-rate Flash into something even greater.
Geoff Johns' introduction of the Rogues' code of honor gave Flash's villains a unique identity that set them apart from other villain groups in comics.
The Rogues' code specifically forbids killing speedsters, as they recognize that doing so would bring unwanted attention and escalation from the superhero community.
In Forever Evil (2013-2014), the Rogues fought against the Crime Syndicate's occupation of Earth, showing that the villains had their own sense of honor and territory.
"We don't kill the Flash. He's our Flash. Nobody touches our Flash." — Captain Cold's possessive attitude toward Flash reflects the Rogues' complex relationship with their hero.
The Crime Syndicate of America features Johnny Quick, an evil speedster counterpart to The Flash from Earth-3.
Professor Zoom's yellow costume was originally explained as being Barry Allen's costume that Thawne found in a time capsule and altered using a device to reverse the colors.
The Reverse-Flash can generate negative Speed Force energy that disrupts and degrades regular Speed Force connections in nearby speedsters.
In the TV series, the Time Vault in S.T.A.R. Labs contained a future newspaper from 2024 that chronicled The Flash's disappearance during a crisis.
The newspaper headline "Flash Missing — Vanishes in Crisis" in the Time Vault was a long-running plot thread throughout The Flash TV series.
Gideon, the AI assistant in the Time Vault, was created by Barry Allen in the future and served as an Easter egg connection to DC's Legends of Tomorrow.
"The speed, the strength, the ability to make a difference — you gave it all to me. You saved my life." — Barry Allen expressing gratitude for the Speed Force.
The Flash's costume in the TV series was primarily designed by Colleen Atwood, an Oscar-winning costume designer.
The gold boots on the TV Flash suit were introduced in later seasons, paying homage to the character's classic comic book appearance.
Grant Gustin's Flash suit evolved through at least seven major iterations over the show's nine seasons, each bringing the costume closer to its comic book counterpart.
The Flash ring in the TV series compressed the suit using liquid nanotechnology, a more sci-fi explanation compared to the comics' chemical compression.
"I'm not the Flash because I'm a hero. I'm the Flash because it's who I'm meant to be." — Barry Allen embracing his destiny as The Flash.
Tom Felton (Draco Malfoy from Harry Potter) guest-starred in The Flash Season 3 as Julian Albert, who was unknowingly serving as Doctor Alchemy.
Keiynan Lonsdale portrayed Wally West/Kid Flash in The Flash TV series, later joining the cast of DC's Legends of Tomorrow.
The Flash Season 3 featured a season-long mystery about Savitar's identity, with the reveal that he was a future time remnant of Barry dividing fan opinion.
The Philosopher's Stone in The Flash Season 3 was reimagined as a calcified piece of dark matter from Savitar's time, used to create new metahumans.
"They lost everything because of speedsters like you and me. Because of our hubris." — Savitar to Barry Allen about the consequences of their speed.
The Flash's cross-dimensional vibration technique was first used to travel to Earth-Two in the comic Flash of Two Worlds and became a staple power.
In Season 2, Earth-2 versions of characters provided memorable alternate takes, including a villainous Cisco (Reverb) and a heroic Killer Frost.
Harry Wells of Earth-2, played by Tom Cavanagh, became one of the most popular characters on The Flash, known for his gruff demeanor and genius intellect.
The Council of Wells, a gathering of Harrison Wells variants from across the multiverse, was a comedic highlight of The Flash Season 4.
"Snow my God!" — H.R. Wells' trademark exclamation, one of many catchphrases Tom Cavanagh invented for his various Wells characters.
H.R. Wells from Earth-19 sacrificed himself in the Season 3 finale, using facial transmogrification technology to take Iris's place and be killed by Savitar.
The concept of "time travel sickness" or "temporal psychosis" was introduced in the show to explain the mental toll of frequent timeline alterations.
In the comics, Professor Zoom once rewrote Barry Allen's entire history, making small changes throughout his timeline to make his life progressively worse.
Thawne's alterations to Barry's timeline included causing a friend to die in childhood, making Barry miss important events, and ensuring his mother's murder.
The Flash Museum in the comics has been both a tourist attraction and a plot device, with villains frequently breaking in to steal technology.
"The future isn't written yet. It's whatever we make of it, so make it a good one." — A philosophy that resonates with The Flash's time-travel themes.
Trajectory was the first female speedster villain on The Flash TV series, a scientist who created a serum replicating super speed with dangerous side effects.
The Flash Season 4 attempted a lighter tone after the darker Season 3, with Neil Sandilands' DeVoe providing a cerebral villain contrast.
Amunet Black, played by Katee Sackhoff, was a recurring villain in The Flash Season 4 who controlled metallic shards.
The Flash entered the Speed Force prison in the Season 3 finale, staying trapped for six months before being pulled out at the start of Season 4.
"This house is bitchin'." — Barry Allen's bizarre statement upon emerging from the Speed Force, initially seeming like nonsense but later revealed to be a clue.
The Thinking Cap, worn by various characters in The Flash Season 4-5, boosted intelligence but carried the risk of dark matter corruption.
Ralph Dibny's stretching powers on The Flash TV series required extensive CGI work, making him one of the show's most effects-heavy characters.
Cecile Horton, played by Danielle Nicolet, gained empathic powers during her pregnancy in Season 4, eventually becoming a full member of Team Flash.
The Flash Season 5's Cicada represented a departure from speedster villains, using a power-dampening dagger as his primary weapon.
Chris Klein portrayed Cicada/Orlin Dwyer in Season 5, a non-metahuman who gained abilities from a dark matter-infused lightning bolt shard.
Grace Gibbons, Cicada's niece, became the second Cicada from the future, continuing her uncle's anti-metahuman mission.
"The only thing standing between you and your goal is the story you keep telling yourself." — Harrison Wells offering motivational wisdom to Barry Allen.
Nora West-Allen's Season 5 arc involved secretly working with Eobard Thawne in the future, creating conflict when Team Flash discovered the betrayal.
The Flash Season 5 finale saw Thawne freed from his prison in 2049 after Nora erased Cicada's dagger, the device dampening his powers.
The Flash has been parodied in numerous shows, including Robot Chicken, Family Guy, The Big Bang Theory, and Saturday Night Live.
Sheldon Cooper on The Big Bang Theory dressed as The Flash for Halloween, contributing to the character's mainstream pop culture recognition.
The Flash's iconic hand-vibrating-through-chest move, used to threaten or kill opponents, has been depicted in comics, the TV series, and films.
Eobard Thawne killing Cisco by vibrating his hand through his chest in The Flash Season 1 was one of the show's most shocking and graphic moments.
Time travel in The Flash follows loose rules, with changes to the timeline creating "time remnants" rather than paradoxes in most cases.
"Time travel. It's a hell of a drug." — Cisco Ramon summarizing the complicated and addictive nature of timeline manipulation.
The Flash can generate an electromagnetic pulse by running in circles fast enough to create a rapidly shifting magnetic field.
Speed thinking has been depicted as Flash perceiving the world in extreme slow motion, with everyone around him essentially frozen in place.
In the comics, Wally West once rebuilt an apartment building from rubble in seconds by vibrating the molecules back into their original positions.
The Flash can vibrate his vocal cords at super speed to disguise his voice, a technique Barry uses to hide his identity in the TV series.
"Nanites, courtesy of Ray Palmer." — Oliver Queen's tactical statement about the nanite arrow, which became one of the most memed lines in the Arrowverse.
The Flash's ability to run on clouds and even through space using the Speed Force has been depicted in several comic storylines.
In the DC Comics event Blackest Night, Barry Allen returned fully to life and fought alongside the other heroes against the Black Lantern Corps.
The Black Lantern versions of dead Flash villains attacked Central City during Blackest Night, including a zombified Captain Boomerang.
The Brightest Day storyline that followed featured the return of Captain Boomerang (Digger Harkness), setting up a new rivalry with The Flash.
"I've spent my whole life running and running to try and get to the impossible. I forgot that the people right in front of me are who matter most." — Barry Allen's Season 1 realization.
The Flash's time-travel mechanics in the TV series were influenced by the "bootstrap paradox" — a causal loop with no clear origin point.
The Flash has broken the time barrier by exceeding Mach 10 in various comic depictions, though the exact speed requirement has varied.
The Scarlet Speedster is one of The Flash's most common nicknames, referring to his signature red costume.
The Crimson Comet is another popular nickname for The Flash, used mainly in comic book narration and by other characters.
The Sultan of Speed, the Monarch of Motion, and the Vizier of Velocity are among the more creative nicknames given to The Flash over the decades.
"I love being The Flash. I love every second of it. And I'm going to keep loving it even when it's hard." — Grant Gustin speaking about his time in the role.
The Flash's lightning bolt chest emblem has been merchandised on everything from t-shirts to coffee mugs to phone cases, making it one of the most recognizable superhero logos.
The Flash's distinctive red and yellow color scheme was chosen to make him visually striking and distinguishable from other heroes on the comic page.
In the Flashpoint timeline, Aquaman's Atlantis sank Western Europe while Wonder Woman's Amazons conquered the United Kingdom.
The Flashpoint version of Batman (Thomas Wayne) was far more brutal than Bruce Wayne's Batman, willing to use guns and kill criminals.
Thomas Wayne's letter to Bruce in Flashpoint, delivered by Barry Allen, became one of the most emotionally powerful moments in DC Comics history.
"Thank you, Barry." — Bruce Wayne's tearful reaction to reading his father's letter from the Flashpoint timeline, establishing Flash and Batman's deep friendship.
The Flashpoint storyline has been adapted multiple times beyond the comics, including the animated film, the TV series, and the 2023 movie.
In the animated series The Flash (1967), The Flash appeared as part of The Superman/Aquaman Hour of Adventure on CBS.
The Super Friends animated series (1973-1986) featured The Flash as a recurring character, introducing him to a generation of young viewers.
Challenge of the Super Friends (1978) featured The Flash as a core member fighting against the Legion of Doom.
The Flash appeared in the Justice League: Crisis on Two Earths animated film, voiced by Josh Keaton.
In the animated film Justice League: The New Frontier, The Flash was voiced by Neil Patrick Harris, bringing his trademark charm to the role.
The Flash made a notable appearance in the Teen Titans animated series, with the show featuring Kid Flash (voiced by Michael Rosenbaum) in later episodes.
In Teen Titans, Kid Flash's flirtatious relationship with Jinx became a beloved subplot, showing his ability to see the good in people.
The Flash has appeared in every season of the Arrowverse, making him the most consistently present hero across the shared universe.
"The future's not set. There's no fate but what we make for ourselves." — A time-travel philosophy that resonates throughout The Flash's storytelling.
The Flash's super-speed punch delivered at near-light speed would theoretically carry the energy equivalent of a nuclear explosion.
Scientists have calculated that if The Flash ran at the speed of light, the friction would heat the air around him to plasma temperatures.
The Speed Force conveniently solves many physics problems that would make super-speed impossible, including air friction, momentum, and sonic booms.
In real physics, running at The Flash's depicted speeds would create shockwaves equivalent to continuous sonic booms, devastating the surrounding area.
The Flash comics have explored the scientific implications of super-speed more seriously than most superhero comics, making the character appealing to science enthusiasts.
DC Comics published a book called "The Science of The Flash" that examined the real-world physics behind the character's abilities.
The Flash is frequently cited in physics classrooms as a way to discuss concepts like relativity, friction, time dilation, and the speed of light.
"Speed is my drug. The more I have, the more I want." — Zoom's addiction to speed mirrored real-world addiction themes in a superhero context.
The Flash ring prop from the TV series became one of the most popular pieces of Arrowverse merchandise, sold as replicas to fans.
Grant Gustin continued to use social media to interact with Flash fans long after the show ended, maintaining a strong connection with the fandom.
The Flash TV series' take on the multiverse directly influenced the DCEU's approach to multiverse storytelling in The Flash (2023) movie.
The Speed Lab in S.T.A.R. Labs served as Team Flash's training facility, where Barry would test the limits of his abilities.
The Pipeline, a converted particle accelerator tunnel beneath S.T.A.R. Labs, was used to imprison metahuman criminals on The Flash TV series.
The ethics of imprisoning metahumans in the Pipeline without trial was a source of fan debate throughout The Flash TV series' run.
"We're supposed to be the good guys. How is locking people up without a trial any different from what the bad guys do?" — A moral question raised about Team Flash's methods.
The Flash TV series incorporated real Central City landmarks into its fictional geography, blending real and imagined locations.
Big Belly Burger is a fictional fast-food chain that appeared frequently in The Flash and other Arrowverse shows as a recurring background detail.
Jitters, the coffee shop where Iris worked early in the series, became an iconic location on The Flash TV series, named with a caffeine-speed double meaning.
CC Jitters sold a "Flash" coffee drink on the show, playing into the character's celebrity status within Central City.
The Flash's identity as Central City's beloved hero contrasts with Batman's more feared vigilante status, reflecting the two characters' fundamentally different approaches.
"Barry Allen is The Flash, the superhero who protects Central City. But first and foremost, he's a good man." — Joe West describing what makes Barry special beyond his powers.
The Flash's origin story — ordinary person struck by extraordinary circumstances — has been cited as one of the most relatable in superhero fiction.
Unlike Batman or Iron Man, The Flash doesn't rely on wealth or technology. His powers are inherently part of him, making him a truly self-made hero.
The Flash family concept — multiple generations of speedsters supporting each other — is unique in comics and represents the power of legacy.
The passing of the Flash mantle from Jay to Barry to Wally to Bart represents one of the most celebrated legacy traditions in superhero comics.
"Every generation improves upon the last." — Jay Garrick's belief in the Flash legacy, trusting each successor to carry the mantle further.
In the DC Universe, being called "The Flash" carries immense weight, as the name represents not just speed but hope, sacrifice, and heroism.
The Flash's Central City is often depicted as a bright, optimistic city, reflecting the character's hopeful nature — in contrast to Gotham's darkness.
The Flash has been a breakfast cereal mascot, appeared on lunchboxes, and been featured in countless kids' products, making him one of DC's most marketable heroes.
The Flash's popularity in merchandise consistently ranks alongside Batman, Superman, and Wonder Woman as one of DC's top four characters.
"Hope is not a feeling. Hope is a choice. And I choose to hope." — Barry Allen's optimistic philosophy, even in the darkest times.
The Flash TV series' particle accelerator explosion scene was recreated in miniature for the show's opening credits throughout its run.
Lightning effects on The Flash TV series were created using a combination of practical lighting effects and CGI enhancement.
The Flash's running sequences in the TV series used a combination of treadmill filming, wire work, and CGI speed trails.
The slow-motion "Flash time" sequences became one of the most distinctive visual elements of The Flash TV series.
"Flash Time" became the title of Season 4 Episode 15, which took place almost entirely in a fraction of a second from Barry's super-speed perspective.
The Flash entered the Speed Force at the end of Season 3 as penance for creating Flashpoint, spending months in a timeless dimension.
When Barry returned from the Speed Force in Season 4, he initially spoke in seemingly random phrases that were later revealed to be prophecies.
"We're gonna need more diapers." — One of Barry's Speed Force prophecies that seemed nonsensical but eventually proved meaningful.
Clifford DeVoe's plan in Season 4 involved using the Enlightenment satellite to reboot humanity's intelligence, essentially removing free thought.
The Thinker's Enlightenment was stopped when Barry threw the satellite into orbit, but a piece landed in Central City, creating the Cicada dagger.
Season 4's humor-focused approach divided fans, with some enjoying the lighter tone and others preferring the more dramatic earlier seasons.
The Council of Wells comedic subplot featured Tom Cavanagh playing multiple versions of himself simultaneously, showcasing his versatility.
"Every myth, every legend, every story of hope — they all include a hero who is willing to make the sacrifice." — Barry Allen on the nature of heroism.
The Flash comics have explored themes of grief extensively, particularly through Barry Allen's inability to accept his mother's death.
Wally West's Flash run explored themes of self-doubt and imposter syndrome, as he constantly measured himself against the legacy of Barry Allen.
Bart Allen's tenure as The Flash explored themes of youth and responsibility, showing a young hero struggling with expectations.
The Flash's rogues gallery is notable for featuring blue-collar, working-class villains rather than megalomaniacal world-conquerors.
The Rogues consider themselves professional criminals rather than supervillains, treating their conflict with Flash as a job rather than a crusade.
"We have rules. And the first rule is: we don't kill the Flash." — Captain Cold establishing the Rogues' code in the comics.
Captain Cold's backstory involves an abusive father and a troubled childhood, making him one of the more sympathetic villains in Flash's rogues gallery.
Heat Wave's pyromania is depicted as a genuine psychological condition in the comics, adding depth to what could be a one-dimensional villain.
Mirror Master's Mirror Dimension has been depicted as a parallel reality where reflections live independent lives, adding horror elements to Flash stories.
The Turtle's ability to slow everything around him makes him uniquely dangerous to The Flash, as his powers directly counteract super speed.
"Speed isn't about running fast. It's about being there when people need you." — A distillation of The Flash's heroic philosophy.
In DC Comics, speedsters have their own unique relationship with death. Many who die are absorbed into the Speed Force rather than truly dying.
The Speed Force serves as both a power source and an afterlife for speedsters, with deceased speedsters existing as energy within it.
Max Mercury spent centuries bouncing through different time periods, unable to fully enter the Speed Force, until the 20th century.
Johnny Quick used a mathematical formula (3X2(9YZ)4A) to access the Speed Force, suggesting that speed power could be accessed through mathematics.
The Speed Force has been described as the source of all motion in the DC Universe, making it a fundamental force of reality.
In some interpretations, the Speed Force exists because Barry Allen exists — his future self generated the lightning that created both him and the Speed Force in an infinite loop.
"The Speed Force chose you for a reason." — A sentiment expressed to various speedsters, suggesting the force has its own will and purpose.
Inertia, a clone of Bart Allen, served as a dark mirror to Impulse, eventually orchestrating the events that led to Bart's death as The Flash.
The Dark Flash saga featured Walter West, an alternate Wally West from a darker timeline who temporarily replaced the main Wally.
The Flash: Iron Heights one-shot by Geoff Johns explored the prison that housed the Rogues, depicting it as a horrific facility.
Blacksmith, a female villain who could merge organic and inorganic matter, served as the leader of the New Rogues in Geoff Johns' Flash run.
The Flash Season 7 opened with a shortened storyline due to Season 6's early end from the pandemic, creating an unusual narrative structure.
The Forces storyline in Season 7 introduced new forces — the Strength Force, Sage Force, and Still Force — as characters with human avatars.
Psych (Sage Force), Fuerza (Strength Force), and Deon Owens (Still Force) became recurring characters in The Flash's later seasons.
The Flash Season 8 opened with a five-episode "Armageddon" event featuring appearances from multiple Arrowverse heroes.
Despero served as the primary villain of the "Armageddon" event, an alien warlord who believed The Flash would destroy the world.
"Armageddon" featured the Reverse-Flash creating a "Reverse-Flashpoint" timeline where Thawne was the hero and Barry was the villain.
"I will always be your Reverse." — Eobard Thawne declaring his eternal opposition to Barry Allen.
Brandon Routh appeared as Ray Palmer/The Atom in The Flash Season 8's "Armageddon" event, reuniting with his Legends of Tomorrow role.
The Flash Season 9 introduced Red Death, a villainous speedster version of Ryan Wilder (Batwoman) from an alternate Earth.
Red Death in The Flash Season 9 was a female version of the character, differing from the comics' Bruce Wayne-based Red Death.
The Red Death in DC Comics was a Bruce Wayne who forcibly merged with the Barry Allen of his world, combining Batman's intellect with Flash's speed.
Dark Nights: Metal (2017) introduced the Dark Multiverse and several nightmare versions of Batman, including the Red Death.
The Flash Season 9's final villain arc involved a new version of Cobalt Blue, tying back to Barry Allen's family history.
Cobalt Blue in the comics is Malcolm Thawne, Barry Allen's secret twin brother who was separated at birth and raised by the Thawne family.
The revelation that Barry Allen and Eobard Thawne are connected through family (Malcolm Thawne being raised by Thawnes) adds another layer to their rivalry.
"I'm the fastest man alive. And I'm surrounded by people who make me better." — Barry Allen acknowledging that his greatest strength is his support system.
The Flash finale featured cameos from numerous past cast members, giving fans a nostalgic send-off for the long-running series.
The final scene of The Flash TV series showed Barry Allen continuing to run, leaving his story open-ended rather than conclusive.
Greg Berlanti's Arrowverse began with Arrow in 2012 and The Flash became its most popular spinoff, spawning multiple additional series.
The Flash TV series was the highest-rated Arrowverse show for most of its run, consistently drawing the network's largest audiences.
The Flash Season 1 is widely regarded as the best season of the series, with its Reverse-Flash mystery and emotional storytelling.
"It was me, Barry! I was the one who did all those terrible things!" — Eobard Thawne's reveal as the Reverse-Flash in Season 1 became an iconic meme.
The "It was me, Barry" meme transcended the show, becoming a widely used internet meme format applied to countless situations.
The Flash's cultural impact extends beyond entertainment — the phrase "in a flash" has become synonymous with extreme speed in everyday language.
The character of The Flash has inspired real scientific research into the theoretical limits of human speed and the physics of super-speed.
DC Comics' speedsters have inspired discussions in physics journals about time dilation, the speed of light, and theoretical faster-than-light travel.
"You showed me what it's like to have a son. And I am grateful for that." — Harrison Wells/Eobard Thawne showing genuine emotion toward Barry despite being his enemy.
The complex relationship between Barry Allen and Eobard Thawne is considered one of the best hero-villain dynamics in superhero fiction.
Thawne's genuine affection for Barry, combined with his pathological need to be his enemy, creates a uniquely tragic villain dynamic.
In The Flash Season 1, Thawne-as-Wells trained Barry to become faster specifically so Barry could generate enough speed to send Thawne home to the future.
The selfish yet functional mentorship between disguised-Thawne and Barry made their conflict personal in a way few hero-villain relationships achieve.
"Sometimes great possibilities are right in front of us, and we don't see them because we choose not to." — Harrison Wells/Eobard Thawne.
The Flash's Speed Force connection has been depicted as both a blessing and a curse, with the power sometimes threatening to consume the speedster.
In comics, Wally West once nearly lost himself to the Speed Force during the Terminal Velocity storyline, saved only by his love for Linda Park.
Linda Park, Wally West's wife, served as his "lightning rod" — an emotional anchor that kept him from being lost in the Speed Force.
The concept of a "lightning rod" — a person who grounds a speedster in reality — has been expanded across Flash comics and the TV series.
Iris West-Allen served as Barry's lightning rod in the TV series, their love repeatedly helping Barry find his way back from the Speed Force.
The Flash can generate Speed Force constructs in some comic depictions, creating solid objects from pure speed energy.
The Speed Force has been visualized as containing the spirits of all speedsters who have ever existed, past, present, and future.
In DC Comics lore, the Speed Force was one of the fundamental forces created at the dawn of the universe, alongside other cosmic forces.
The Flash's powers have evolved over 80+ years of publication, with modern versions being significantly more powerful than Golden Age depictions.
"I don't need anyone to save me. I just need someone to believe in me." — Barry Allen expressing the emotional core of his character.
Flash comics have won multiple Eisner Awards, recognizing the consistently high quality of storytelling in the title.
Mark Waid's Flash run is frequently cited in lists of the greatest comic book runs of all time.
Geoff Johns' Flash: Rogue War storyline brought all of Flash's villains together in an explosive conflict that redefined the Rogues.
Francis Manapul and Brian Buccellato's New 52 Flash run was praised for its innovative artwork and kinetic page layouts.
Manapul's art on The Flash used creative panel layouts that incorporated speed lines and motion into the very structure of the page.
The Flash is one of only a handful of DC characters to have maintained a continuous or near-continuous comic series since the Silver Age.
"The world doesn't need a Flash who's perfect. It needs a Flash who never gives up." — A sentiment that captures the character's enduring appeal.
The Flash's optimism and determination have made him a symbol of hope in the DC Universe, often compared to Superman in terms of inspirational value.
While Superman represents hope through power and invulnerability, The Flash represents hope through perseverance and the willingness to keep running.
The Flash has been featured in DC's Future State event, showing potential futures where new heroes take up the Flash mantle.
In DC's Future State, Jess Chambers served as The Flash, representing a non-binary future version of the speedster legacy.
The Flash has crossed over with characters outside DC, including a memorable crossover with Marvel's Quicksilver.
In the DC vs. Marvel crossover (1996), The Flash (Wally West) raced against Quicksilver and won, establishing DC's speedsters as faster.
The Flash and Quicksilver's rivalry represents the broader DC vs. Marvel speedster debate that has persisted among fans for decades.
The Amalgam Comics crossover merged Flash with Ghost Rider to create Speed Demon, a bizarre combination of the two characters.
"I'm just a regular guy who was struck by lightning. But that lightning changed everything." — Barry Allen on the transformative nature of his accident.
The Flash Fandom is one of the most dedicated in comics, with fans maintaining extensive wikis, databases, and discussion forums.
Flash Day is celebrated annually by fans on the anniversary of Flash Comics #1's publication in January 1940.
The Flash has appeared in over 3,000 individual comic book issues across various series, one-shots, and crossovers.
Central City has been described as "the city that runs" — a motto that reflects both The Flash's presence and the city's energetic character.
The Flash's rogues gallery is considered the third-best in comics, behind Batman's and Spider-Man's, due to the depth and consistency of its villains.
"What makes the Rogues special isn't their powers. It's that they're professionals." — Geoff Johns explaining why Flash's villains are unique.
Captain Boomerang's son, Owen Mercer, briefly served as the second Captain Boomerang and had limited super-speed inherited from his father's connection to the Speed Force.
The Top once possessed the body of Henry Allen's attorney in the comics, demonstrating his evolved telekinetic and possession abilities.
Double Down is a Flash villain whose cursed playing cards are embedded in his skin and can be peeled off as razor-sharp weapons.
Plunder is a mirror-universe version of a Keystone City detective who crossed over to the main DC universe to become a bounty hunter villain.
Fallout is a Flash villain who involuntarily emits lethal radiation, making him dangerous to everyone around him including himself.
"It's not the speed that makes you a hero. It's what you do with it." — Jay Garrick imparting wisdom to younger speedsters.
The Flash TV series' version of Central City was depicted as a thriving metropolis with a distinctive skyline and waterfront.
The S.T.A.R. Labs set on The Flash TV series was one of television's largest standing sets, containing the Cortex, Speed Lab, and Pipeline.
The Cortex served as Team Flash's central command center, with its distinctive circular console becoming an iconic piece of Arrowverse production design.
The Flash TV series employed a team of over 500 people at its peak, including a large visual effects department for super-speed sequences.
The Flash suit on the TV series weighed approximately 40 pounds in early seasons, with later iterations being lighter and more flexible.
Grant Gustin trained extensively for the role, maintaining a rigorous fitness regimen throughout the show's nine-season run.
The Flash TV series addressed themes of PTSD, depression, and mental health through Barry Allen's struggles with trauma and loss.
"Being The Flash doesn't just mean having powers. It means having the courage to use them." — Barry Allen inspiring the next generation.
The Flash's time remnant concept was one of the TV series' most debated plot devices, with fans arguing about the logic of temporal duplicates.
Savitar's existence as a discarded time remnant raised philosophical questions about identity and whether duplicates deserve equal consideration.
The Flash TV series finale received mixed reviews, with fans appreciating the emotional closure but some feeling the final season was rushed.
"Every ending is also a beginning." — The Flash TV series' thematic message in its final episodes, honoring the legacy while looking forward.
The Flash's legacy in television extends beyond ratings — the show helped establish the template for the modern superhero TV universe.
Without The Flash's success on The CW, shows like Supergirl, Legends of Tomorrow, Black Lightning, and Batwoman might never have been greenlit.
The Flash TV series proved that comic book-accurate costumes, super-powered villains, and cosmic storylines could work on a TV budget.
The Arrowverse crossover events, anchored by The Flash, became annual television spectacles that drew millions of viewers.
"In the end, I'm not the fastest man alive. I'm just the one who never stopped running." — Barry Allen in a reflective moment about what truly defines The Flash.
The Flash's influence on pop culture can be seen in countless references across media, from music to movies to internet culture.
The phrase "pulling a Barry Allen" has become fan shorthand for making things worse by trying to fix them through time travel.
The Flash emoji (⚡) is widely used on social media to represent speed, energy, and the character himself.
DC Comics has published multiple Flash-centric events, including Flash War, Speed Metal, and The Flash Age, demonstrating the character's importance to the publisher.
The Flash's popularity has made him a staple of DC theme park attractions, including rides at Six Flags and Warner Bros. Movie World.
The Flash Coaster at Six Flags is one of several Flash-themed rides at the park, combining super-speed theming with roller coaster thrills.
"You want to be a hero? Then be a hero. Don't wait for permission." — Barry Allen encouraging others to take action.
THE FLASH has appeared in LEGO sets since 2012, with multiple versions of the character available as minifigures across various DC LEGO themes.
Hot Toys has produced highly detailed Flash action figures based on both the TV series and the movie, prized by collectors.
The Flash's enduring appeal lies in his relatability — unlike godlike heroes, Barry Allen is fundamentally a good person who happened to gain extraordinary abilities.
Jay Garrick's continued presence in DC Comics, even 85+ years after his creation, speaks to the timeless quality of The Flash concept.
The Flash family's multigenerational nature makes it one of the most inclusive and diverse superhero legacies in comics.
"The fastest man alive isn't the one who runs the fastest. It's the one who never stops." — A thematic summary of what The Flash represents in the DC Universe.
